key_insight: The paper introduces a lowest-order mixed finite element method for strain gradient elasticity that uses a physically meaningful third-order double-stress variable to achieve robust, parameter-stable discretization.
engineering_relevance: It provides an efficient way to simulate gradient-enhanced solid mechanics without high-order bubble enrichment or Nitsche treatment, making it attractive for practical multiscale and boundary-layer problems.
method: Model the double stress as a third-order tensor primary unknown, \u03a6 = i^2 grad σ(u).
Approximate \u03a6 with an S\u2297R^d-valued lowest-order Raviart–Thomas-type element and displacement with vector-valued Crouzeix–Raviart.
Add local quadratic post-processing and a hybridized formulation to improve usability and implementation.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports parameter-robust stability, first-order convergence for fixed parameters, and a uniform O(i^{1/2}+h) error bound, supported by numerical experiments in 2D and 3D.
watch_item: The method targets lowest-order accuracy, so practical performance may still depend on mesh resolution near boundary layers and on implementing the tensor-valued mixed/hybrid formulation correctly.
paper_title: A Lowest-Order Robust Mixed Finite Element Method with a Third-Order Tensor Variable for Strain Gradient Elasticity
